Current exercise-science references on isometric joint-angle specificity, muscle length, intent, youth supervision, and breathing/Valsalva risk
cluster.scoringRule: Isometrics is the dominant methodology on every card. Strength, body_control, balance, coordination, and resilience scores vary by exercise intent; explosiveness is only secondary for high-intent overcoming isometrics.

cards.name: Front Plank
cards.cardSummary: Prone trunk hold that teaches anti-extension, rib-pelvis stacking, and whole-body tension without motion.
cards.bestPlacement: Use after the warm-up as trunk control prep, in Resilience after main work, or as a low-fatigue bridge before crawling or loaded bracing.
cards.description: Front Plank is an isometric exercise using the prone forearm plank to develop core, spine, shoulder control, tissue tolerance, and force expression without visible joint movement.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: trunk_pelvis_anti_movement_control. Coach exact joint angles, gradual tension, steady breathing, and a clean stop. Isometrics are useful only when the athlete owns the position rather than surviving the clock.
cards.athleteLanguage: Own the position, build steady pressure, breathe, and stop before the shape changes.
cards.whyItWorks: Front Plank removes visible movement so the athlete can create force, organize posture, and feel the target tissues at a precise joint angle. The static demand makes compensation easier to see and lets the coach scale intensity, range, and duration without adding impact.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in resilience / trunk_pelvis_anti_movement_control because the primary training problem is isometric position ownership for core, spine, shoulder. It should be placed where it supports readiness, force production, or resilience without stealing quality from faster work.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not turn the hold into a breath-holding contest, pain-tolerance challenge, or fatigue finisher. The set is over when alignment, breathing, or target-tissue tension is lost.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale isometrics first by position quality, then by hold duration, then by external load or intent. A harder hold is not better if it forces pain, breath holding, or compensation.

movementRequirements.postural_shape: prone forearm plank
movementRequirements.breathing_demand: quiet nasal or controlled open-glottis breathing

coachingExecution.movement_description: Set up in the prone forearm plank. Create tension gradually, hold the exact position, breathe under control, and end the set before posture, pain-free range, or breathing quality breaks.
setup: Set the athlete in the prone forearm plank.
Choose the easiest variation that allows pain-free alignment and calm breathing.
Coach a gradual build of tension instead of a sudden jerk into the position.
execution_steps: Create the target body shape and confirm the key joints are aligned.
Build pressure into the floor, implement, wall, band, cable, bar, or body position as appropriate.
Hold for about 20 seconds or the prescribed effort window while breathing under control.
End the set as soon as alignment, breathing, pain-free range, or target-tissue tension changes.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: Rep counts only when the athlete holds the chosen angle with steady pressure, controlled breathing, and no visible compensation.
stop_signs: Pain, pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or headache
Breath holding that cannot be corrected
Alignment changes or shaking becomes uncontrolled
Unsafe equipment, anchor, wall, rack, or landing setup

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the easiest stable version of Front Plank: shorten the hold to 5-10 seconds, add wall/bench/coach support, and stop every rep while breathing and position are still clean.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use 10-20 second holds with clear start/stop standards. Add range, lever length, or light load only when the athlete can keep alignment and breathing consistent.
scaling.teen: Progress by joint angle, lever length, load, or intent, but keep quality gates strict. Do not use max-effort isometrics when the teen is fatigued from sprinting, jumping, or heavy lifting.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with supported positions, moderate effort, and longer rest. Choose a joint angle the athlete can own without pain, compensation, or breath holding.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with longer holds, stronger intent, heavier load, or more specific joint angles. Use full rest for high-intent efforts and avoid stacking too many hard isometrics in one session.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, low-to-moderate effort, and shorter holds. Avoid breath holding, dizziness, or positions that create fall risk; prioritize confidence and clean breathing.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use comfortable positions, avoid prolonged breath holding or high intra-abdominal pressure, reduce hold duration, and choose side-lying, seated, or supported alternatives when needed.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender adjustment; scale by training age, symptoms, joint tolerance, strength level, pregnancy/postpartum status, and current readiness rather than gender alone.
doNotUseWhen: Pain appears in the target joint or tissue
Athlete cannot maintain alignment or breathe during the hold
The setup is unstable or the athlete lacks a safe exit
The session priority is fresh max-speed, jump, or power output and this hold would create fatigue first

Choose the easiest variation that allows pain-free alignment and calm breathing.
Coach a gradual build of tension instead of a sudden jerk into the position.
execution_steps: Create the target body shape and confirm the key joints are aligned.
Build pressure into the floor, implement, wall, band, cable, bar, or body position as appropriate.
Hold for about 4 seconds or the prescribed effort window while breathing under control.
End the set as soon as alignment, breathing, pain-free range, or target-tissue tension changes.
common_faults: Unsafe pin height or no spotter plan
coachingExecution.quality_gate: Rep counts only when the athlete holds the chosen angle with steady pressure, controlled breathing, and no visible compensation.
stop_signs: Pain, pinch, numbness, tingling, dizziness, or headache
Breath holding that cannot be corrected
Alignment changes or shaking becomes uncontrolled
Unsafe equipment, anchor, wall, rack, or landing setup

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the easiest stable version of Bench Press Pin Iso: shorten the hold to 5-10 seconds, add wall/bench/coach support, and stop every rep while breathing and position are still clean.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use 10-20 second holds with clear start/stop standards. Add range, lever length, or light load only when the athlete can keep alignment and breathing consistent.
scaling.teen: Progress by joint angle, lever length, load, or intent, but keep quality gates strict. Do not use max-effort isometrics when the teen is fatigued from sprinting, jumping, or heavy lifting.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with supported positions, moderate effort, and longer rest. Choose a joint angle the athlete can own without pain, compensation, or breath holding.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with longer holds, stronger intent, heavier load, or more specific joint angles. Use full rest for high-intent efforts and avoid stacking too many hard isometrics in one session.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable support, low-to-moderate effort, and shorter holds. Avoid breath holding, dizziness, or positions that create fall risk; prioritize confidence and clean breathing.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use comfortable positions, avoid prolonged breath holding or high intra-abdominal pressure, reduce hold duration, and choose side-lying, seated, or supported alternatives when needed.

cards.description: Split-Stance Cable Pallof Iso Hold is an isometric exercise using the split stance Pallof press-out hold to develop core, hip, knee control, tissue tolerance, and force expression without visible joint movement.
